name: cursedspace

metadata.name: cursedspace
metadata.summary: Library for TUI programs on basis of curses

documentation:
# cursedspace

A python library/framework for TUI application on the basis of the curses
package.


## Example use

Here’s a very simple example of how to use the cursedspace package:

    #!/usr/bin/env python3

    import curses
    from cursedspace import Application, Key, Panel, colors


    class DemoApplication(Application):
        def __init__(self):
            super().__init__()
            self.panel = None

        def main(self):
            self.panel = Panel(self)
            self.resize()

            self.screen.addstr(0, 0, "Just some color example", colors.attr(colors.RED))

            while True:
                curses.doupdate()

                key = self.read_key()

                if key == Key.RESIZE:
                    self.resize()
                elif key in [Key.ESCAPE, "q", "^C"]:
                    break

        def resize(self):
            height, width = self.size()
            self.panel.resize(height, width)
            self.panel.paint()


    # run the application
    DemoApplication().run()

For more examples see the `examples` folder.

## Components

 * `Application` is the main application class and provides boilerplate
   initialisations
 * `Panel` is a basic panel with support for borders and key handling in the
   context of an `Application`
 * `InputLine` is a panel with very basic editing support.
 * `Key` provides a convenient wrapper around curses’ key system. It can be
   used standalone even when you don’t want to use `Application` or `Panel`.
 * `ShellContext` is a convenient wrapper to execute external processes (e.g.
   through subprocess) and returning to the curses context afterwards again.
 * `colors` is a generic way to define colors in pairs (foreground and
   background) and have them automatically registerd for use in curses. You
   only have to call `colors.attr(ColorPair(...))` to use a new color
   combination (limited by the terminal, of course).

classes.name: cursedspace.application.Application

methods.name: cursedspace.application.Application.refresh
documentation:
Refreshes the screen

If force is set to True, it will touch the screen first
to enforce a full refresh

methods.name: cursedspace.application.Application.size
documentation: Refreshes the size of the screen and returns the (height,width) tuple

methods.name: cursedspace.application.Application.main
documentation: Your entry point for the main loop

methods.name: cursedspace.application.Application.read_key
documentation:
Read the next key press from the system

Returns a cursedspace.Key instance

methods.name: cursedspace.application.Application.run
documentation: The main function to call when actually running the application

methods.name: cursedspace.application.Application.show_cursor
documentation:
Whether or not to show the cursor

This is a wrapper for curses.curs_set, but will remember the original
state of the cursor and restore it when the application ends, so you
don't have to.

methods.name: cursedspace.application.Application.init_curses
documentation:
Will be called prior to using any curses operations in self.run()

Normally you should not have to call this.

methods.name: cursedspace.application.Application.end_curses
documentation:
Will be called after the end of the application

This function will restore the terminal and cursor state prior
to the launch of the application and clear the screen.

methods.name: cursedspace.application.Application.set_term_title
documentation: Attempt to set the title of the terminal emulator

A generic application with curses

Use self.screen for drawing operations.

classes.name: cursedspace.shellcontext.ShellContext
documentation:
Conveniently allows you to run a subprocess in curses shell mode

Example usage:

```
# in a cursedspace.Application
with ShellContext(self.screen):
    subprocess.run(...)
self.paint(True)
```

classes.name: cursedspace.progress.ProgressBar
documentation: A generic progressbar that can be updated.

classes.name: cursedspace.panel.Panel

methods.name: cursedspace.panel.Panel.__init__
documentation:
app is the cursedspace.Application this panel belongs to
size is a tuple (height, width)
pos the position on the application's window (y, x)

methods.name: cursedspace.panel.Panel.destroy
documentation:
Should be called explicitely when closing a panel to restore
the window(s) "behind" this panel.

methods.name: cursedspace.panel.Panel.handle_key
documentation:
Panel specific call-back when a key has been entered by the user

key is of type cursedspace.Key

methods.name: cursedspace.panel.Panel.resize
documentation: Resize the panel

methods.name: cursedspace.panel.Panel.refresh
documentation:
Refresh the panel

force will touch the panel's content prior to refreshing

methods.name: cursedspace.panel.Panel.content_area
documentation:
The area available for content, i.e. inside the borders

Returns a tuple (y, x, height, width)

methods.name: cursedspace.panel.Panel.focus
documentation: Move the cursor into this panel

methods.name: cursedspace.panel.Panel.paint
documentation:
Draws the panel into the curses buffer

Will draw a border based on what self.border is set to.
If clear is True, it will erase any content of the window first.

methods.name: cursedspace.panel.Panel.move
documentation: Move the panel
A generic panel with or without border

classes.name: cursedspace.grid.Grid

methods.name: cursedspace.grid.Grid.fill
documentation: Fill the Grid with panels

methods.name: cursedspace.grid.Grid.add_panel
documentation:
Add a panel to the grid.

cols and rows denote the grid-position of the panel and 
can either be integer indecies or a slice to span 
multiple grid-cells.

Returns the panel object that is being stored in the grid.

Args and kwargs denote the additional arguments to supply 
to the panel instansiation.

methods.name: cursedspace.grid.Grid.remove_panel
documentation: Remove a panel from the grid.

methods.name: cursedspace.grid.Grid.resize
documentation: Resize the grid

methods.name: cursedspace.grid.Grid.refresh
documentation: Refresh the grid

methods.name: cursedspace.grid.Grid.paint
documentation: Paint the grid
A generic grid of panel-type objects that allows for any rectangular tiling.

functions.name: cursedspace.colors.as_colorpair
documentation: Try to turn value into a ColorPair

functions.name: cursedspace.colors.register
documentation:
Register a color pair to be used in curses later

Returns True upon success, False upon failure (logs to 'warning')

functions.name: cursedspace.colors.attr
documentation:
Return a curses-like attribute value for 'color'

'color' may be one of the Colors constants or a tuple
(foreground, background) of Colors constants.

functions.name: cursedspace.colors.init_colors
documentation: Initializes the basic foreground colors and sets up color pairs for each

classes.name: cursedspace.scrollpanel.ScrollPanel

methods.name: cursedspace.scrollpanel.ScrollPanel.selected_item
documentation: The currently selected item

methods.name: cursedspace.scrollpanel.ScrollPanel.do_paint_item
documentation:
Here is where you define how to paint that item on the window

y, x: position where to draw the item
maxwidth: available space on that line
is_selected: whether or not that item is the currently selected one
item: the item to paint

methods.name: cursedspace.scrollpanel.ScrollPanel.jump_to
documentation:
Select this item by instance or by index

Puts the cursor on this item and returns True (you have to call
paint yourself; most likely with clear=True).
Returns False if the item is not in the list or the index was out
of range.

methods.name: cursedspace.scrollpanel.ScrollPanel.handle_key
documentation:
Handle user input key

Returns True if handled, False otherwise

methods.name: cursedspace.scrollpanel.ScrollPanel.select_next
documentation:
Move the cursor to the next item and update the representation

Returns True if repaint required, otherwise False

methods.name: cursedspace.scrollpanel.ScrollPanel.select_previous
documentation:
Move the cursor to the previous item and update the representation

Returns True if repaint required, otherwise False

methods.name: cursedspace.scrollpanel.ScrollPanel._select_by_index
documentation:
Move the cursor to item with index

Return True if repaint is required, False otherwise

If False is returned the list may or may not have updated
and repainted the old and the new element.

methods.name: cursedspace.scrollpanel.ScrollPanel.handle_scrolling_keys
documentation:
Can be called to handle scrolling keys

If a scrolling key is found, it will likely update self.cursor.

May call self.scroll (which updates self.offset).
May call self.paint_item (and self.do_paint_item subsequently).

Returns a tuple (handled, must_repaint, must_clear) meaning this:
    * handled: the key was a scrolling key
    * must_repaint: you should call self.paint
    * must_clear: you should pass clear=True to self.paint

A generic panel that allows for scrolling content (e.g. a list)

The content to scroll is per line in self.items.

After updating the content of self.items you may want to reset
self.offset and self.cursor to point to a valid element in the list of items.
Or just set self.cursor and self.scroll to determine the self.offset
automatically.

ScrollPanel.SCROLL_MARGIN defines when the list should start scrolling.
ScrollPanel.SCROLL_PAGING defines whether or not the scrolling should be paging
  instead of line by line.
ScrollPanel.SCROLL_NEXT and ScrollPanel.SCROLL_PREVIOUS are lists of valid Keys
  that cause the selection of the next/previous item respectively. These can
  trigger scrolling.

To control how an item is displayed, override the do_paint_item function.

modules.name: cursedspace.inputline
documentation: A single line of user input with optional completion

functions.name: cursedspace.inputline.SingleWordCompletionWrapper
documentation: Convenience wrapper for SingleWordCompletion

classes.name: cursedspace.inputline.Completion

methods.name: cursedspace.inputline.Completion.handle_key
documentation:
When InputLine receives a key, it will call this function

Be sure to return False if you do not consume the key, because
only then will InputLine handle the key itself.

methods.name: cursedspace.inputline.Completion.alternative_selected
documentation: Called when the user selected an alternative from the list of options

methods.name: cursedspace.inputline.Completion.update
documentation:
Called by the InputLine when the user changed the text

`y` and `x` are the absolute coordinates of the cursor
in respect to the app.screen window.

To access the current text and the cursor position in the
text, refer to `self.inputline.text` and `self.inputline.cursor`.
Or get the current word through `self.inputline.current_word`.

In this function you must compile the possible suggestion alternatives
and call `self.set_alternatives(alternatives, y, x)`.
Afterwards you will have to call `self.app.paint`, too.

Or, if no alternatives can be suggested, make sure to call
`self.close`.

methods.name: cursedspace.inputline.Completion.autosize
documentation: Auto resize the suggestion box with the anchor being at y,x

methods.name: cursedspace.inputline.Completion.close
documentation:
Close the suggestions panel

Returns True if the suggestion box was destroyed, False if nothing was done

class_variables.name: cursedspace.inputline.Completion.MAX_HEIGHT
documentation: Maximum height of the suggestion window

class_variables.name: cursedspace.inputline.Completion.MAX_WIDTH
documentation:
Maximum width of the suggestion window

-1 means that it tries to fit the longest suggestion.

A completion/suggestion box for InputLine

This allows you to add autocompletion/suggestions to an InputLine
component.

You will have to derive your own variant from this though and implement
`update`.

Have a look at the sample `SingleWordCompletion` to see what that could
look like.

classes.name: cursedspace.inputline.SingleWordCompletion
documentation:
An example implementation of a completion

This will provide autocompletion/suggestion for the first word
that the user types/might type in the input line.

To use it, pass SingleWordCompletionWrapper(list_of_words) to the input
line like this:

    words = ["brick", "elbow", "pudding"]
    edit = InputLine(self, self.size()[1], (0, 0),
                     completion=SingleWordCompletionWrapper(words))

or instead assign the `completion` property afterwards:

    edit = InputLine(self, self.size()[1], (0, 0))
    edit.completion = SingleWordCompletion(edit, words)

You can also use Completion.Suggestion to select by label and insert
other text:

    suggestions = [Completion.Suggestion(*pair)
                   for pair in [("Spanish Inquisition", "nobody"),
                                ("I'm not dead!", "parrot")]]
    edit.completion = SingleWordCompletion(edit, suggestions)

classes.name: cursedspace.inputline.InputLine

methods.name: cursedspace.inputline.InputLine.scroll
documentation: Recalculate 'offset' based on window size and cursor position

methods.name: cursedspace.inputline.InputLine.resize
documentation: Change the width of the input field

methods.name: cursedspace.inputline.InputLine.current_word
documentation:
Return the span of the current word (the word at the cursor)

Returns a tuple (start, end) or None if not on a word.

methods.name: cursedspace.inputline.InputLine.replace_word
documentation:
Replace the word at the cursor position with `word`

If the cursor is on a space, the word is inserted before the cursor.

Unless `move_to_end` is set to `False`, the cursor will be moved
to the end of the word at the end of the replacing.

methods.name: cursedspace.inputline.InputLine.absolute_cursor
documentation: Return the (y,x) tuple of the absolute cursor position on screen

methods.name: cursedspace.inputline.InputLine.update_completion
documentation: Trigger .update on the completion component, if there is any

A panel that allows for user input as a text input field does.

Example use case:

```
# in your Application's run function:
edit = InputLine(self, self.size()[1], (0, 0))

while True:
    key = self.read_key()
    if key == Key.RESIZE:
        edit.resize(self.size()[1])
    elif key == Key.RETURN:
        # user accepted input, check edit.text
        break
    elif key == Key.ESCAPE:
        # user cancelled input
        break
    else:
        edit.handle_key(key)
```

The input line allows the use of a completion box. To use it, just provide
an instance of Completion (you will have to derive your own though):

> class MyFancyCompletion(Completion):
> : def update(self, y, x):
>
>   : self.set_alternatives(["fancy"], (y, x))
>
> edit = InputLine(self, self.size()[1], (0, 0), completion=MyFancyWordCompletion)

Or assign the InputLine.completion parameter later to your instance:

> edit = InputLine(self, self.size()[1], (0, 0))
> edit.completion = MyFancyCompletion(edit)
